coredata.dc:title: Dual-emitting carbon dots as a self-ratiometric fluorescent probe for the determination of pantoprazole in pharmaceutical dosage forms
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Kamal, Amira H.
Kannouma, Reham E.
Hammad, Mohamed A.
Mansour, Fotouh R.
coredata.dc:description: Dual-emission carbon dots provide an efficient and straightforward approach for constructing ratiometric fluorescent probes using carbon dots alone. This approach exploits the two distinct emission bands of dual-emission carbon dots, eliminating the need for additional luminescent components. In this study, we introduce nitrogen and phosphorus-doped carbon dots synthesized from glucose and diammonium hydrogen phosphate as a novel and environmentally friendly ratiometric fluorometric probe for quantifying pantoprazole in pharmaceutical formulations. The nitrogen and phosphorus-doped carbon dots were synthesized via a rapid, one-step microwave process completed in 1.5 min. When excited at 300 nanometers, these carbon dots produced dual fluorescence emissions at 355 nanometers and 425 nanometers. Pantoprazole effectively quenched the fluorescence of these carbon dots due to the inner filter effect and static quenching via complex formation. Utilizing these dual emissions as reference peaks, this spectrofluorometric approach was validated according to International Council for Harmonization standards. The sensor displayed a strong linear response over a concentration range of 0.50 to 35.00 µg per milliliter, with a detection limit of 0.16 µg/mL and recoveries between 99.91 and 100.36 % (relative standard deviation <0.2 percent). Demonstrating greater environmental friendliness than traditional high-performance liquid chromatography and ultraviolet methods, this approach received favorable environmental ratings through MoGAPI and AGREE, presenting a sustainable, economical, and time-efficient analytical method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microwave synthesis
Green analytical chemistry
Ratiometric
Pantoprazole

Application of the developed method for the assay of PAN in pharmaceuticals
Comparative analysis of previously published studies
Ethics approval and consent to participate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image (inset: histogram of particle size distribution), (B) FTIR, (C) EDX chart, and (D) 3D excitation-emission matrix of PN@CDs.

(A) Co-plot of UV/Vis spectrum of PAN and the fluorescence excitation and emission of PN@CDs, (B) the quenching efficiency ( %E) using corrected and observed fluorescence of PN@CDs in the presence of different concentrations of PAN (n = 3), (C) Stern-Volmer plot at different temperatures (n = 3), and (D) The overlay of UV/Vis absorption spectra of PN@CDs, and a mixture containing PN@CDs and PAN (with PAN as a blank).
The fluorescence spectra of PN@CDs after the addition of different PAN concentrations from 0.50µg/mL to 35.00µg/mL.
The calibration plots of PAN through plotting of (A) F°/F355, (B) F°/F425, (C) I425/I355 on the y-axis against PAN concentrations (µg/mL) on the x-axis (n = 3).

The modified green analytical procedure index (MoGAPI) of the developed approach (A) and the reported HPLC one (a). The analytical greenness metric approach (AGREE) of the developed approach (B) and the reported HPLC one (b).
Analytical merits of the developed ratiometric fluorescence method for the determination of PAN.

row.entry: λ ex − λ em1, λ em2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Assessment of accuracy, precision (intraday and inter-day) of the suggested method for the determination of PAN.
Application of the developed method for the determination of PAN in pharmaceuticals and statistical evaluation against the reported HPLC method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Pantaloc®: each tablet contains 40 mg of PAN.
Protofix®: each vial contains 40 mg of PAN.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The listed t-test and F-test values at a significance level of 0.05.

head.ce:title: Dual-emitting carbon dots as a self-ratiometric fluorescent probe for the determination of pantoprazole in pharmaceutical dosage forms

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Tanta, 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, Tanta, 31111, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Sadat City, Sadat City, Monufia, 32958, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Sadat City, Sadat City, Monufia, 32958, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Elgeish Street, the medical campus of Tanta University, Tanta, Egypt 31111.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Elgeish Street, the medical campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual-emission carbon dots provide an efficient and straightforward approach for constructing ratiometric fluorescent probes using carbon dots alone. This approach exploits the two distinct emission bands of dual-emission carbon dots, eliminating the need for additional luminescent components. In this study, we introduce nitrogen and phosphorus-doped carbon dots synthesized from glucose and diammonium hydrogen phosphate as a novel and environmentally friendly ratiometric fluorometric probe for quantifying pantoprazole in pharmaceutical formulations. The nitrogen and phosphorus-doped carbon dots were synthesized via a rapid, one-step microwave process completed in 1.5 min. When excited at 300 nanometers, these carbon dots produced dual fluorescence emissions at 355 nanometers and 425 nanometers. Pantoprazole effectively quenched the fluorescence of these carbon dots due to the inner filter effect and static quenching via complex formation. Utilizing these dual emissions as reference peaks, this spectrofluorometric approach was validated according to International Council for Harmonization standards. The sensor displayed a strong linear response over a concentration range of 0.50 to 35.00 µg per milliliter, with a detection limit of 0.16 µg/mL and recoveries between 99.91 and 100.36 % (relative standard deviation <0.2 percent). Demonstrating greater environmental friendliness than traditional high-performance liquid chromatography and ultraviolet methods, this approach received favorable environmental ratings through MoGAPI and AGREE, presenting a sustainable, economical, and time-efficient analytical method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Pantoprazole (PAN), chemically known as 5-difluoromethoxy-2-yl-3,4-dimethoxypyridyl methyl sulfoxide, is a benzimidazole-based compound and a widely used proton-pump inhibitor that treats various acid-related conditions, including gastroesophageal reflux disease [1]. Like other proton-pump inhibitors, PAN reduces gastric acid production by binding to and inhibiting the H +/K + ATPase proton pump found in the stomach lining's parietal cells [2]. When exposed to low pH environments, PAN converts to its active form, a tetracyclic cationic sulfenamide, which then interacts with specific cysteine residues on the proton pump, ultimately decreasing acid secretion [2]. Licensed in 2001, this medication is effective in managing conditions like Zollinger-Ellison syndrome and preventing recurrence of ulcers [3]. Additionally, proton-pump inhibitors have shown antimicrobial properties against Helicobacter pylori [3].

Various analytical methods for determining PAN in biological fluids and pharmaceuticals have been reported in recent years. These include HPLC [4–6], LC-MS/MS [7,8], TLC [9,10], spectrophotometry [11,12], and electrochemistry [13–15]. However, several of these approaches have drawbacks such as high costs, time-consuming sample pretreatment, and arduous detection system preparation. They are additionally complicated to apply. Problems with this method include high detection limits, the inclusion of hazardous solvents (low greenness), extended reaction times, and unstable colored products. These limitations limit their benefits for routine PAN assays.

Nanomaterial-based fluorescence sensors are gaining popularity for chemical analysis due to their ease of use, speed, sensitivity, and selectivity [16–20]. There are limited spectrofluorimetric methods for the analysis of PAN, based on micelle-enhanced fluorescence [21], and quenching of the nanomaterials fluorescence [22–24].

Carbon nanoparticles offer a novel approach to fluorescence sensing with improved functional materials [25–27]. The novel fluorescent carbon nanomaterial known as carbon dots (CDs) has a wide range of applications in the fields of catalysis, solar cells, chemo/biosensing, bioimaging, and biotherapy [28,29]. Its advantages include low toxicity, photobleaching resistance, favorable biocompatibility, easy functionalization of the surface, low cost, and easy synthesis [30–32]. Most of the CDs used in chemical sensing that have been described thus far are single wavelengths, and the analysis results can be influenced by some variables, including the environment, the source of light, the concentration of the probe, and the equipment [33,34]. The ratiometric fluorimetry (FL) method is a widely used approach to address the aforementioned problems and enhance the reliability and accuracy of analyte detection. The design of ratiometric fluorescent probes depends on changes in emission intensity across two or more spectral bands triggered by the presence of an analyte [35]. There are various ways to manufacture CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence probes, including composite formation, and dual-emitting CD tactics. The mechanical mixing tactic involves blending CDs with various optical materials and dyes, rare-earth metal ions, QDs, and metal nanoclusters by simple stirring. The term "nanohybrid tactic" describes the covalent or non-covalent assembling of CDs and other optical materials into a nanocomposite. CDs with two emission bands can be utilized directly as ratiometric FL probes, eliminating the need for additional optical materials. The dual-emitting CDs approach offers a very efficient and straightforward method for building ratiometric FL probes based on CDs. This technique, which simply depends on the two emission bands of dual-emitting CDs, eliminates the need for the incorporation of extra luminous units [36].

Herein, for the first time, a dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescent CD probe (PN@CDs) was fabricated through microwave pyrolysis in 90 s and applied as a sensor for PAN determination. At 300 nm excitation, the PN@CDs showed two emission peaks at 355 and 425 nm. The two emission peaks were quenched in the presence of PAN in a ratiometric manner correlated to PAN concentration. It was found that the quenching was mainly due to the inner filter effect and static quenching via complex formation. More significantly, good recoveries and RSD were obtained in the ratiometric sensing of PAN using PN@CDs in pharmaceutical dosage forms (enteric coated tablet and vial for injection). This self-ratiometric probe can normalize the signal, so the analysis is less susceptible to any interference from other sources of variation, including variations in the excitation source. Additionally, a comparison of the probe's accuracy with HPLC's detection results of PAN in pharmaceutical dosage forms was made.

Pantoprazole sodium sesquihydrate (purity 99.80 %) was kindly provided by Zeta Pharma Company (Sadat City, Egypt). Methanol and phosphoric acid were bought from Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany). Glucose, hydroxy propyl methyl cellulose (HPMC), diammonium hydrogen phosphate, sodium hydroxide, lactose, acetic acid, tartaric acid, boric acid, silicon dioxide, mannitol, and magnesium stearate were bought from Piochem Co. (6th of October, Egypt).

Pantaloc® enteric coated tablets with batch no 232,733 (Medical Union Pharmaceuticals Co., Ismailia, Egypt). The label states that each tablet contains 40 mg of PAN. Protofix® vial with batch no 240,086 (Rameda Co., 6th of October, Egypt). The label states that the vial contains 40 mg of PAN. An aqueous solution was prepared using deionized water.

A Thermo Scientific Evolution 201 double-beam UV/Vis spectrophotometer (USA) was connected to a PC with Thermo Scientific Insight Pro software to collect UV/Vis absorption spectrums. The spectrofluorometer used for recording fluorescence was the JASCO FP-8300 from Tokyo, Japan, with slit widths of 5 nm, 1000 nm/min scanning speed, sensitivity (medium), and a quartz cell of 1-cm. Software from Jasco Co., Tokyo, Japan called Spectra Manager was used to process and acquire data. A Talos F200i (S)TEM from Thermo Fisher Scientific in the USA, operating at 200 kV acceleration voltage, was used to analyze the morphology of PN@CDs. A Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometer (Tensor 27, Burker, Germany) was utilized to investigate the surface functionalization of PN@CDs. For the synthesis of PN@CDs, a household Tornado Microwave (TM-25MK, 900 W) was utilized. A Jenway 3510 pH meter from Cole-Parmer (Saint Neots, UK) was used to measure pH. A Singen, Germany-based Elmasonic S 60 H ultrasonic cleaning device was utilized. A purifying system (SUEZ, Paris, France) was employed to deionize water.

For the synthesis of PN@CDs, 500 mg of Glucose and 700 mg of diammonium hydrogen phosphate were put in a beaker and dissolved in a volume of 20 mL of deionized water then sonicated for 5 min. The microwave heated the combination for ninety seconds at 900 W (high power). The produced brown-black residue was mixed with 50 mL of deionized water before sonication for five minutes. A 0.22 μm syringe filter was used for the filtration of the supernatant. This filtered solution was stored in the fridge for more than a month without experiencing any precipitation before it was used.

The quantum yield (QY) of the PN@CDs was assessed with by the single-point method [37].
(1)
Q
Y
P N @ C D s
= Q
Y
Q S
X
( F
L
P N @ C D s
/ F
L
Q S
)
X
( A b
s
Q S
/ A b
s
P N @ C D s
)
(
η
P N @ C D s
/
η
Q S
)
where FL refers to the integrated fluorescence intensity, Abs refers to the absorbance and η refers to the refractive index. The reference was quinine sulfate (QS). Quinine sulfate was prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4 (at 350 nm, QYQS was 0.54). To standardize the impact of the absorbance, the absorbance value was adjusted to 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of stock and working solutions
ce:section.ce:para: In a 10 mL flask, 10 mg of PAN was dissolved in methanol and the volume was completed to the mark with the same solvent to obtain a 1 mg/mL stock solution. Various volumes of the standard stock solution were added to 60 µL of PN@CDs in a 10 mL volumetric flask and the volume was completed to the mark with deionized water to get final PAN concentrations within the range of 0.50–35.00 μg/mL. Emission intensities were recorded at 355 nm and 425 nm after excitation at 300 nm. A fluorescence intensity ratio of I425/I355 versus the concentration of PAN in μg/mL was used to produce the calibration curve. All data were the average of triplicate measurements.

Ten Pantaloc® enteric-coated pills were precisely ground and weighed in this instance. In a 100 mL flask, a precise amount of powder corresponding to 40 mg of PAN was dissolved in methanol with the aid of sonication for twenty minutes, and the volume was completed to the mark with the same solvent (0.40 mg/mL). The resulting stock solution was centrifuged for 10 min at 6500 rpm, and a volume of the supernatant was added to 60 μL PN@CDs and deionized water was added to adjust the volume to 10 mL to get the specified working solutions. While the Protofix® vial was directly dissolved in 100 mL methanol with no further treatment. It was subjected to the same procedures as the tablets. After calculating the percentage recoveries, the results were compared to those obtained using the HPLC method outlined before [6].

A self-ratiometric spectrofluorimetric determination of PAN was selected in the proposed study. The method offers simplicity, speed, sensitivity, and cost-effectiveness and it has not been applied before for PAN estimation. In this work, a green, eco-friendly and simple preparation of PN@CDs was performed by a microwave-assisted method using glucose and diammonium hydrogen phosphate as starting materials. These two specific precursors were selected after extensive experimentation with various precursors. Glucose, sucrose, oxalic acid, glycine and thymol were selected as carbon sources. Other precursors like urea, ammonium acetate and diammonium oxalate were selected as nitrogen dopants, while dipotassium hydrogen phosphate and diammonium hydrogen phosphate were selected as phosphorus dopants. Through several trials, it was observed that glucose and diammonium hydrogen phosphate precursors consistently produced CDs with well-defined and distinct dual-emission peaks, making them suitable for ratiometric fluorescence. Additionally, they demonstrated superior stability, higher quantum yield, and reproducible optical properties compared to other tested precursors, making them the optimal choice for this application.

The Characterizations of the produced PN@CDs were obtained using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM), FT-IR, Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), UV/Vis spectrophotometry, and spectrofluorometry. HRTEM analysis revealed that PN@CDs had a well-defined spherical form with particle sizes varied from 0.8 to 3.5 nm, with an average of 2 nm (Fig. 1 A).

The prepared PN@CDs' chemical structure was examined using FTIR. The spectra (Fig. 1B) displayed a large peak at 3416 cm−1, indicating the presence of stretching vibrations related to the -N-H and -O-H bonds. The stretching vibration of -C-H may be responsible for the peak at 2852 cm−1. The peak at 1461 cm−1 indicated the existence of CC and CN, while the peak at 2101 cm−1 could be attributed to CN stretching. CO, CO-C, PO, and P-O-R could be responsible for the peaks located at 1646, 1355, 1157, and 999 cm−1, respectively [38,39]. These results showed that the surface of the manufactured PN@CDs had multiple function moieties, including carboxylic, amine, hydroxyl, and phosphate, which promoted their water solubility. EDX elemental analysis revealed that the PN@CDs were largely constituted of C, O, N, and P at 18.6 %, 54.5 %, 15.9 %, and 10.9 %, respectively (Fig. 1C). This indicates successful high doping.

To investigate the optical properties of the PN@CDs, UV/Vis spectrophotometers and fluorescence spectrofluorometers were used to record the PN@CDs absorption and fluorescence spectra, respectively. Figure S1 clearly shows an absorption peak in the UV/Vis spectrum at about 270 nm, which is thought to be related to the aromatic sp2 bond's π → π* transition [40,41]. By investigating the 3D excitation-emission spectra of the synthesized PN@CDs (Fig. 1D), it was noticed that it produced two maximum emission bands at 355 nm and 425 nm after being excited at 300 nm. Then, the quantum yield of the prepared PN@CDs was measured to be found 8.9 % at 330 nm excitation.

When PAN was present, the fluorescence intensity of PN@CDs at both emission bands (355 nm, 425 nm) was considerably reduced. To maximize the responsiveness of the synthesized PN@CDs to PAN, various experimental parameters were examined and modified, including the PN@CD volume, pH, and incubation period. Being the main emission peak, F°/F355 was used as a guide to get the optimum condition where F° and F are the fluorescence intensity of the PN@CDs in the absence and the presence of PAN, respectively at 355 nm.

Five levels (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 µL) were employed to figure out the optimum volume of PN@CDs. In a 10 mL flask, each level was mixed with a preset amount of PAN standard solution to obtain a concentration of 10µg/mL and the volume was completed to the mark with deionized water. It was discovered that 60 µL of the PN@CDs (Figure S2A) produced the maximum fluorescence quenching, which was then applied to additional optimization.

It is well known that the pH of the solution can change the interactions that occur between CDs and the quencher as well as the fluorescence intensity of the CDs [42]. In order to examine how pH affected the reaction between the produced PN@CDs and PAN, a broadly applicable Britton-Robinson buffer (0.02 M) was used to change the pH within the range of 2–10 [43]. The impact of pH was studied with and without PAN, and F°/F355 was determined. The quenching effect of PAN was found to be reduced when pH was changed with a buffer, and the greatest quenching was achieved when pH was left unchanged (Figure S2B). Consequently, the devised process gained additional benefits as it could be performed using deionized water, negating the need for pH adjustment in later steps.

The duration of incubation was investigated due to its noteworthy impact on the process of quenching. Fluorescence spectra were obtained after the addition of PAN to PN@CDs. The effectiveness of quenching in the presence of PAN was measured at various intervals ranging from 0 to 45 min. After 15 min, the optimum fluorescence quenching was achieved, and as the reaction time was increased, no more quenching was observed (Figure S2C).

When PAN was present, the fluorescence of PN@CDs was considerably decreased. Fluorescence quenching mechanisms can be broadly classified into three categories: inner filter effect (IFE), dynamic quenching, and static quenching. When the quencher absorbs either the emission light or the excitation light, IFE occurs. In spectrofluorimetric studies, IFE is usually a problematic source, but new studies have revealed that it can be utilized to develop innovative fluorescence assays [44]. The IFE was anticipated to happen in this study due to the overlap between the UV/Vis absorption spectrum of PAN (the quencher) and the excitation spectrum of the PN@CDs (Fig. 2 A). Following the addition of PAN, Eq. (2) was used to eliminate IFE from the fluorescence of PN@CDs.
(2)
F
L
c o r r
= F
L
o b s
x
10
(
A e x + A e m
)
/ 2
where Aem and Aex stand for, respectively, the quencher absorbance at the emission(355 nm) and excitation (300 nm) wavelengths. FLobs and FLcorr stand for corrected and observed fluorescence intensity, respectively.

The suppressed efficiency ( %E) for the corrected and observed fluorescence was determined using Eq. (3):
(3)
% E =
[
1 −
(
F /
F
∘
)
]
x
100
where %E represents the suppressed efficiency, F° is the PN@CDs fluorescence intensity, and F is the FLcorr or FLobs.

The %E of the corrected and observed fluorescence intensities of PN@CDs is plotted versus PAN concentrations in micromolar in Fig. 2B It is discovered that the primary mechanism by which PAN quenches the fluorescence of PN@CDs is the IFE. Stern–Volmer analysis was used to examine the contributions of various quenching mechanisms, such as static and dynamic quenching, at various temperatures. Using the Stern-Volmer's equation (Eq. (4)), the potential quenching mechanism was deduced.
(4)
F ∘
F
= 1 + K s v
[ Q ]
= 1 + K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
where F° and F represent the fluorescence emission intensities at 355 nm without and with PAN, respectively. Ksv refers to the Stern–Volmer constant, while [Q] is the concentration of PAN. Kq denotes to the quenching rate constant, and τ0 is the average lifetime (10−8 s). Stern–Volmer relationship was examined at three different temperatures, starting from room temperature with 10 °C increments (303, 313 and 323 K) as shown in Fig. 2C. It was observed that increasing the temperature caused the Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) to decrease, indicating that static quenching works as a second quenching process in addition to the IFE [45]. Additionally, the static mechanism of quenching was verified by calculating Kq values from the measured Ksv values, which were found to be 2.96×1012 L mol−1 s−1, bigger than 2.0 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1 (the maximum diffusion rate constant) [46]. Additionally, using PAN as a blank, Fig. 2D demonstrated a disruption in the UV/Vis spectra of the PN@CDs, suggesting the creation of a ground state complex through the formation of hydrogen bonds and electrostatic attraction between surface function groups of PAN and PN@CDs and verifying the static quenching mechanism [46].

Based on LOD=3σ/S (where S is the slope of the linear calibration plot and σ is the standard deviation of the intercept), the limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.16 µg/mL. The Limit of quantitation was 0.50 µg/mL. Table 1 demonstrates the analytical parameters of the developed methodology.

The average percentage recoveries of triple PAN concentration determination at three different levels (5, 15, and 30 µg/mL) were calculated to evaluate the accuracy of the suggested method. The estimated percentage recoveries, which varied from 99.91 to 100.36 %, demonstrated how accurate the method was. Simultaneously, the precision (intra and inter-day) was expressed as relative standard deviation, and it was discovered to be not >0.20, demonstrating the designed approach's repeatability (Table 2 ).

The method's selectivity was studied by checking the impact of possible interfering materials on the emission of the fabricated PN@CDs. It was noted that PAN quenched the fluorescence of PN@CDs significantly in comparison with other materials (Figure S3A). PAN was tested in the presence of a range of excipients, such as lactose, mannitol, magnesium stearate, silicon dioxide, tartaric acid, and HPMC, to exclude excipient interference. The results show that the excipients had no effect, indicating a high selectivity for the planned procedure (Figure S3B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the developed method for the assay of PAN in pharmaceuticals
ce:section.ce:para: PAN in Protofix® vial and Pantaloc® enteric coated tablets was measured using the suggested approach. Calculations were done to determine the relative standard deviation (RSD) and average percentage recoveries. Furthermore, formulations were examined according to the reported HPLC methodology [6], as shown in Figure S4. When the two approaches were statistically compared using the t-test and F-test, the findings showed that, at the 95 % confidence level, there were no significant differences (Table 3 ). This shows that the ratiometric spectrofluorometric approach developed to determine PAN in pharmaceuticals is suitable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative analysis of previously published studies
ce:section.ce:para: To provide a more objective performance evaluation, the proposed method was systematically compared to previously reported techniques (Table S1). The findings clearly demonstrated that the proposed approach offered a simpler and more sensitive determination, yielding lower limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) than previously established methods. Notably, this technique eliminates the need for costly instrumentation, such as HPLC, and does not require pre-derivatization or complex electrode fabrication.

While HPLC-UV remains a widely utilized technique for the determination of PAN, it presents several inherent limitations, including high operational costs, labor-intensive sample preparation, prolonged analysis times, substantial solvent consumption, and the necessity for highly trained personnel. In contrast, the proposed spectrofluorometric method offers a cost-effective, efficient, and user-friendly alternative. It provides comparable performance while enabling rapid analysis with minimal resource consumption. Furthermore, the spectrofluorometric approach yields validated results without requiring analyte elution, making it a more environmentally sustainable alternative to conventional chromatographic techniques.

The proposed spectrofluorometric method was assessed using both MoGAPI [48] and AGREE and compared to the reported HPLC procedure. MoGAPI provided a detailed explanation of various analytical practices, including sample preparation, handling, chemicals used, and instruments. Each item was assigned a distinct color, from green to yellow to red, to reflect whether it had a low, medium, or high ecological impact. This modified tool also assigns a total score based on overall performance, facilitating comparison between methods. The method developed attained higher MoGAPI scores compared with the reference HPLC method, as shown in Figs. 5 A and 5a. To confirm this conclusion, the greenness profile was assessed using the AGREE software, with a total score of 0.75 for the spectrofluorometric approach and 0.56 for the HPLC, indicating the benefit of the suggested methodology regarding greenness (Figs. 5B and 5b). The combined use of MoGAPI and AGREE provided a robust evaluation of the environmental impact, which clearly demonstrated the superiority of the proposed spectrofluorometric method in terms of sustainability and ecological friendliness.

This study introduced, for the first time, a ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor that uses green PN@CDs to determine PAN in pharmaceutical dosage forms with high greenness, sensitivity and selectivity without the need for previous derivatization or pH manipulation. The PN@CDs were made using inexpensive, ecologically benign, and easily accessible precursors in a rapid microwave synthesis process lasting 1.5 min. The fabricated PN@CDs were characterized with the aid of HRTEM, FTIR, and EDX to ensure that nitrogen and phosphorus were doped. Based on the IFE and static mechanism, PAN quantitatively quenched the fluorescence emission of the fabricated PN@CDs. The ratiometric probe used the fluorescence intensity ratio of the PN@CDS/PAN system (I425/I355) as the analytical response ratio. Calibration plots were constructed by plotting this ratio against PAN concentration. A good linearity was obtained over a concentration range of 0.50–35.00 µg/mL, with a low detection limit (0.16 µg/mL). The ratiometric probe accurately determined PAN levels in enteric-coated tablets and vials, with good percentage recoveries and low RSD values. Furthermore, a thorough assessment of the recommended method's greenness was conducted, and the results revealed that, in comparison to the reported HPLC methodology, the proposed approach had a minimal effect on the environment as well as it saved cost and time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable

Amira H. Kamal: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Reham E. Kannouma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mohamed A. Hammad: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Fotouh R. Mansour: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing interests

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public or commercial sectors.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.greeac.2025.100262.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of long-term administration of pantoprazole on bone mineral density in young male rats
Pharmacol. Reports

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Matuszewska, B. Nowak, M. Rzeszutko, K. Zduniak, M. Szandruk, D. Jędrzejuk, M. Landwójtowicz, M. Bolanowski, M. Pieśniewska, J. Kwiatkowska, A. Szeląg, Effects of long-term administration of pantoprazole on bone mineral density in young male rats, Pharmacol. Reports 68 (2016) 1060–1064. https://doi.org/10.1016/J.PHAREP.2016.06.012.
